Patrick Collison’s advice for people in their 20s: “Work at a place with high standards”
When asked for career advice for people in their 20s, Patrick recalls the book Apprentice to Genius. It follows three generations of scientists that mentored each other and were all extremely successful. It then reflects on what was transferred.
One important thing that the mentees say they learned from their mentors was problem selection. In science, you have to choose problems that are sufficiently important yet not so intractable that you can’t actually make any progress.
The other thing the mentees describe is just learning about high standards and what high standards actually are.
Patrick believes this is a really important point:
“When I talk to people in other domains, this is so frequently the thing that I hear from them. That when they worked with X person or Y organization or in Z environment, they learned what great actually is, and that just permanently changed their sense for what their own standard for their work ought to be.”
He continues:
“So maybe one versions of the ‘what people in their twenties should do’ is get some idea as to the domains you’re interested in, but then figure out where you can learn the highest standards. Where are the highest standards embodied? And where can you go and experience that firsthand?”

Why studying history makes you better at solving modern problems
Most careers are built around finding the right answer quickly. But what if the strength lies not in one answer, but in seeing all the possible ones?
That’s what a history degree forces you to do. You’re trained to hold multiple perspectives at once, to weigh conflicting evidence, and to make sense of messy, incomplete information. It’s not about memorising dates; it’s about learning to map the “why” behind events, and the “how” behind decisions. Those same skills carry over into today’s challenges: where no problem is ever straightforward, and every solution has trade-offs.
I’ve found this to be true in my own journey. The skills I built through studying history—critical analysis, pulling from different sources, and considering outcomes from every angle—have helped me navigate decisions far beyond the classroom. Whether it’s in projects, problem-solving, or strategy, that habit of asking “what else could be true?” often makes the difference.
The biggest takeaway? Careers rarely reward you for having a single neat answer. They reward you for being able to explore the full picture, connect the dots, and make thoughtful choices in uncertain situations.
Because in the end, the best problem-solvers aren’t the ones who know everything. They’re the ones who stay curious enough to keep asking what possibilities they might be missing.

Academia is full of opportunities —committees, reviews, lectures, panels, grants. Each one has value, but saying yes to everything risks exhausting both time and energy.
In this blog, Dr[Becky Carlyle](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/becky-carlyle-bb399118?trk=public_post-text)shares her perspective on the demands of an academic career. Drawing on her experience in both the US and UK, and lessons from elite sport, she explains how clear long-term goals and flexibility are essential for decision making.
She outlines how priorities shift with career stage, why mentors and networks are invaluable, and how learning to say no is not an apology but an act of self-preservation.
Becky's reflections highlight practical ways to stay on track, adapt to setbacks, and maintain ownership of your path.

There’s a big conversation right now around how our education system prepares (or rather fails to prepare) students for the workforce. So I started reflecting: What undergraduate class best prepared me for my current job as a management consultant? The answer was surprising. It wasn’t any of my Econ classes (though they are up there) and it certainly wasn’t any of my business classes (finance was probably the only useful one). If you want to read about why, check out the longer post in my recently launched Substack publication, Learning Well, where I’m exploring trends and fallacies in how we talk about education, seeking to uncover the truth about how humans learn, and reflecting on what it all means for how we approach school, careers, and personal well-being.

Beyond the Grade: Why Reading is Your Unfair Advantage in the Modern Workforce. We've all been there: cramming for an exam, memorizing facts just long enough to secure a passing grade, and then promptly forgetting them. The education system often incentivizes short-term recall over deep, lasting understanding. It's a game, and many of us have learned to play it well.
But here's the reality check the syllabus won't give you: that game ends at graduation.
When you land that dream job, no one will hand you a past paper to memorize. They will hand you a complex problem, a client file, a line of code, a financial report, or a patient case study and say, "What do we do?"
This is where the true value of consistent, engaged reading reveals itself. It's not about passing a test; it's about building a robust mental framework.
·Reading builds competence, not just credentials. Your degree gets you the interview; your knowledge gets the work done. You can't "fake it till you make it" when a project's success depends on your foundational understanding of the field.
·It fosters critical thinking. Books and detailed articles present nuanced arguments and complex systems. This trains your brain to analyze, synthesize, and innovate—skills that are irreplaceable and highly valued.
·It prepares you for the unexpected. The real world is messy and doesn't follow a textbook's chapter outline. A broad knowledge base, built through reading, allows you to adapt and apply concepts to novel situations.
Don't just read to pass. Read to prepare. Your future self—and your future employer—will thank you for the depth you cultivated when no one was awarding points for it.

✨Every question opens a new perspective.
Questions are not just words –they are the keys to curiosity, learning, and meaningful conversations. From What and Why to Do and Is, every question helps us understand ourselves and others better.
Here’s a set of thoughtful questions you can use to spark a conversation about learning, interests, and passions 👇🔹What is your favourite subject?
🔹Why do you like it?
🔹Where did you first learn about it?
🔹When did you start liking it?
🔹Which topic in it excites you most?
🔹Whom do you discuss it with?
🔹With whom do you study it?
🔹How do you prepare for it?
🔹How much time do you spend on it?
🔹How many books do you read for it?
🔹How often do you practice it?
🔹How long have you been interested in it?
🔹Since when have you studied it seriously?
🔹Can you explain why you love it?
🔹Could you tell me who inspired you?
🔹Would you like to make a career in it?
🔹May I know who teaches it?
🔹Where do you study it best?
🔹Do you enjoy solving problems in it?
🔹Did you always like it since childhood?
🔹Does it help you in daily life?
🔹Have you ever scored full marks in it?
🔹Has it inspired you to try something new?
🔹Had you liked it before this school/college?
🔹Was it your favourite in earlier classes too?
🔹Is it useful for your future career?
🔹Who is your favourite teacher for it?
🔹Are you planning to study it further?

A group of successful graduates once visited their former professor.
They had built impressive careers, held prestigious titles, and lived seemingly perfect lives.
While waiting for coffee, their conversation turned to the stresses of work, exhaustion, and the pressures of adulthood.
Some laughed.
Some complained.
Some philosophized.
The professor returned with a tray full of mismatched cups —porcelain and glass, ceramic and plastic, elegant and chipped, minimalist and ornate.
As the guests chose their cups, the professor quietly observed…
Then he spoke:
“Notice how you all instinctively reached for the finest cups —the fancy ones.
Not a single person chose the worn-out or plastic ones.
And that’s the root of much of your stress.”
“You wanted the coffee —not the cup.
But you focused on the cup’s appearance, not the essence of what you came for.”
He continued:
“Life is like coffee.
Career, salary, status, home, car —those are just cups.
They don’t define the richness of life itself.”
“Sometimes we get so distracted by the ‘cup’,
we forget to savor the coffee.”
☕So drink your coffee with joy.
Not by comparing.
Not by competing.
Not by glancing sideways.
Because the happiest people aren’t those who have the best of everything —but those who know how to make the best of what they have.

A group of successful graduates once visited their former professor.
They had built impressive careers, held prestigious titles, and lived seemingly perfect lives.
While waiting for coffee, their conversation turned to the stresses of work, exhaustion, and the pressures of adulthood.
Some laughed.
Some complained.
Some philosophized.
The professor returned with a tray full of mismatched cups —porcelain and glass, ceramic and plastic, elegant and chipped, minimalist and ornate.
As the guests chose their cups, the professor quietly observed…
Then he spoke:
“Notice how you all instinctively reached for the finest cups —the fancy ones.
Not a single person chose the worn-out or plastic ones.
And that’s the root of much of your stress.”
“You wanted the coffee —not the cup.
But you focused on the cup’s appearance, not the essence of what you came for.”
He continued:
“Life is like coffee.
Career, salary, status, home, car —those are just cups.
They don’t define the richness of life itself.”
“Sometimes we get so distracted by the ‘cup’,
we forget to savor the coffee.”
☕So drink your coffee with joy.
Not by comparing.
Not by competing.
Not by glancing sideways.
Because the happiest people aren’t those who have the best of everything —but those who know how to make the best of what they have.

Phone Jail: Literally put my phone in another room. The withdrawal was real, but so were the results.
The 5-Minute Rule: If I could explain a concept in 5 minutes to someone who knows nothing about it, I actually understood it.
Past Paper Goldmine: 90% of my "studying" became solving past papers. Professors are creatures of habit.

Patrick Collison: “Working with people you’re close to is underrated.”
Patrick started Stripe with his brother John and Arc Institute with his wife Silvana. He offers founders the following advice:
“I think working with people you’re close to is underrated… For all of the ventures of any significance in my life, they’ve been with people that I’m very close to and who I had and would like to have an enduring relationship with. Sometimes one hears the advice that you shouldn’t work with friends or your partner, and all of these things are idiosyncratic and there are instances of every possible permutation, but for me it has been a really rewarding experience.”
He continues:
“I think John and I can work together for, you never know, life. But I think we’ll probably work together for decades. For us, it has been both an important source of meaning and fulfillment, but I also think there’s a real complementarity.”

Patrick Collison’s Leadership Turned Stripe Into a $95B Payments Juggernaut. I spent weeks breaking down his interviews, culture docs, and growth playbooks. The result? A masterclass in leadership. Here’s how Patrick’s philosophy turned Stripe into a global payments backbone (and how you can apply it): 1. “Think 100 Years Ahead” (But Execute Tomorrow) Patrick famously tells employees: “Assume Stripe will exist for 100+ years. Now, what’s worth doing today?” How this plays out: Relentless focus on infrastructure: While competitors chased quick wins, Stripe spent years building global compliance systems (now used by 150+ countries). No “growth at all costs”: In 2023, Stripe delayed its IPO to avoid “market noise” and doubled down on profitability. Balance audacious vision with ruthless prioritization. Ask: “Will this decision matter in 5 years?” 2. The “Engineer as CEO” MentalityPatrick (a self-taught coder) leads like a systems architect — scaling culture through principles, not policies. Example: Stripe’s “Write Everything Down” Rule Every major decision is documented in Canonical (Stripe’s internal wiki) Engineers write “bug post-mortems” — Patrick applies this to leadership, publishing “decision autopsies” on failed bets. Why it works: Transparency eliminates tribal knowledge. New hires onboard faster, and missteps become team learnings. 3. The “Talent Density” Obsession Stripe’s hiring bar is Silicon Valley legend. But Patrick’s secret isn’t just hiring A-players — it’s keeping them obsessed. Tactics to steal: “Pre-shipping” projects: Engineers pitch ideas before joining (e.g., a candidate built Stripe’s fraud detection prototype during interviews). No managers for the first 100 hires: Patrick believed early autonomy would attract “self-directed killers.” It worked — Stripe’s first 10 engineers stayed 8+ years. 4. “Default to Open” CommunicationPatrick banned “closed-door strategy sessions” in 2015. How Stripe shares everything: Monthly “Ask Me Anything” docs: Patrick answers 100+ raw questions from employees (e.g., “Why did we cut X feature?”). Customer emails forwarded company-wide: Every complaint/praise is visible, forcing teams to confront blind spots. Result: Stripe’s Glassdoor rating (4.7) beats Google, and attrition is 40% below industry average. 5. The “Arbitrage Learning” Hack Patrick’s reading list for execs (e.g., “Why Nations Fail”) isn’t for show — it’s strategy fuel. His rule: If you solve a problem, share how you learned to solve it. When Stripe struggled with remote work, Patrick studied NASA’s Mars mission team structures. Before expanding to Africa, he spent 3 months interviewing 200+ local founders. Stripe’s $95B valuation isn’t about payments… it’s about leadership that scales thinking, not just headcount. Want to build a company that outlasts market cycles? Patrick Collison on the importance of waiting a really long time to hire people
Everybody tells you “work really hard to hire the best people.” But as Patrick explains, that’s not helpful because everyone knows they should do that. As he puts it:
“The question is to what length should you go and what does that actually mean in practice? And in practice, it means being okay waiting a really long time to hire people.”
It took Stripe six months to hire their first two people, and six more months to hire another 3-4 people. He can think of five people at Stripe who took 3+ years to hire.
“If you think about the smartest people you know, if you want to get them to work on your thing, chances are they already have pretty good paths ahead of them… You have to be way more persistent and be okay with it taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person would think it should take.”
There’s an important compounding effect here —hiring just one great person will make it marginally easier to get the next great person. Patrick argues you should also view every person you hire as bringing along another 50 people just like them if your company is successful:
“Even if they don’t literally hire 50 people, they will be so influential in determining the selection of those 50 people.”
Video source:[Greylock](https://www.linkedin.com/company/greylock-partners?trk=public_post-text)(2015)

Received wisdom is worked really hard to hire the best people and you're like, Gee, thanks. You know, I was going to do the opposite. But now you've said that work really hard to hire the best people. And so I guess the question is sort of what length should you go and kind of what does it actually mean in practice? And in practice it means being OK waiting a really long time to hire people. It took me 6 months to hire the first two people, so a person per quarter, even though we had this kind of network already and you all these people, we've managed like one every three months and then in I think the six months after that. We may be hired another. Three or four people kind of on that order, yeah. You just think about of the smartest people you know and you want to get them to work on your thing. I mean, chances are as far as people you know are working already, yeah, they like they have like pretty good paths out of them, right? There were multiple people that stripe now today who took us several years to hire. I can think off the top of my head of maybe five people who took three plus years to hire. The big thing that people have to keep in mind instead of early stage company recruiting is just you have to be way more persistent. To be OK with taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person you know could think it should take. The vicious cycle is that you're going so slowly when it's the first two or three people and you're like, holy smokes, if we could just get another set of hands and you know, but you don't want to do it. But we talk about this class about compound interest and the value of compounding interest. Like if you get just one great person, right, that makes it marginally easier to get the next great person. So I think that dynamic in spades. And then kind of secondly, I think another way to think about it makes the kind of the importance of it and the potency. That tier is that you have to kind of imagine the tree of sort of all the people this person will hire and going rather than just think about it as kind of do do I want this person or not? It's like, do I want this person and the 50 people who I think they will hire? Because even if they don't literally hire 50 people, they will be kind of so influential in determining sort of the self selection of those 50 people.

We have this joke at Leap - "Speed as a habit - in everything except hiring"
We want to hire slow
As we grow older and wiser as a company, we are getting slower in hiring
The speed of hiring is inversely correlated with the quality of hires you make
It is absolutely okay to wait a really really long time to hire the right person.
And the worst hiring mistakes we have made are the ones where the hiring manager was tired of searching for 2 months and hired the first passably good candidate out of exhaustion and desperation.
Classical sunk cost and local maxima optimisation scenario
Hire slow
Never hire out of exhaustion
Hire Bar-Raisers
Wait for a long time for the right hire

I'm convinced that most tech companies are massively overstaffed. Yet the recent announcements of multiple tech companies to fire 5% of "low-performers" ultimately get it backward.
Overstaffing needs to be fixed with better (and less) hiring and not "better" firing!
Firing 5% of "low-performers" means a) you need to come up with metrics for rating your employees which leads to b) your workforce focusing on gaming those metrics (Goodhart's law).
And don't get me started on natural performance fluctuations that happen in the life of humans (e.g., because of the death of a loved one, break-ups, childbirth,...).
Hire when it hurts, and fire when it really hurts, but not sooner!

5moEdited
We experienced a spike in revenue from the middle of August until today.
Funny enough, if you open our Stripe, it's the first thing you'll notice. 😂How did that happen?
I put arrogance aside and turned it off.
I started receiving a lot of feedback. And when I say a lot, I mean an annoyingly large amount. I ask clients about everything where, when, and how and I record those conversations to share with my team.
And if you think it stopped there, no, it didn't. It went way further.
I know some great people—really great entrepreneurs and marketing experts.
Some of them were my clients, some were friends, and some were just acquaintances.
I actually get on calls or meet with them, telling them everything imaginable about my business:
- Where we are at
- What we are planning to do
- How we are planning to do it
I genuinely enjoy listening to their feedback and taking notes.
Some of them said, "Then why aren't you recording content?"
So, now I record content four times a month.
Some suggested, "Why don't you hire more people?"
So, I started hiring like crazy.
Others even pointed out, "You have a problem in your system—why do you make clients wait for a quote?"
As a result, we added a pricing page at[truggl.com/pricing](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://truggl.com/pricing&urlhash=62C3&trk=public_post-text).
I always take that feedback positively, even if it feels offensive, and use it to grow my business.
I even get messages from young people asking how I do what I do. I tell them it’s simple: you're young, inexperienced, and have nothing to lose.
Open your laptop, learn something, post content every day even if you see no immediate results, sell your services, and congratulations—you’re on the path I took.
(And when I say 'kids,' I mean people my age 😅).

Patrick Collison's (co-founder & CEO at[Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text)) advice for people in their 20's: “𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀”Looking back, the times I learned the most had one thing in common: 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗜𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗲𝗱𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵.
Not just their intelligence, but:
→Did they challenge me?
→Did they push me to grow?
→Did they create an environment where excellence was the norm?
The higher the standards around me, the higher my own baseline became. Those experiences rewired my expectations of what "great work" really looks like.
As Patrick says: “One way to think about your 20s is to explore different domains—but more importantly, find the places where the highest standards exist. Where can you go to experience excellence firsthand?”
Where have you worked that raised your personal standards the most?

And of course you choose something that's, you know, sufficiently important and hard that would be important if you succeeded, but also that. That, you know, it's not so intractable that, you know, you can't actually make any progress. And so the the book describes how this is part of what the mentees described, that, you know, they learned from their mentors. Another thing they talk about is just learning about high standards and what high standards actually are. And when I talk to people in other domains, this is so frequently the thing that I hear from them that when they worked with X person or. And so maybe one version of the, you know, people in the 20s should do is is, you know. Our side: We quickly shipped a change so our reversals would always tie back to the right transaction, regardless of downstream issues—eliminating ambiguity. Patrick Collison on what most startups get wrong pre-product/market fit “The main thing that I think companies screw up at the pre-product/market fit stage is speed of iteration. If you have some kind of meaningful—albeit perhaps small—initial set of users and you’re rapidly iterating in response to their feedback and observed behavior, I think that’s a really good spot to be in… You should be doing everything you can to tighten that feedback cycle.” Patrick believes most startups get this wrong when it comes to hiring — either they hire the wrong people or they hire too many people. Hiring isn’t just expensive financially, but it often makes you move slower. You have to onboard them, get them up to speed, and then there’s a persistent communication and alignment tax that scales quadratically with more team members. Patrick believes the ultimate arbiter for a new hire should be, “Will we be more responsive to what we’re learning about our users given the presence of this additional person?” Empirically the “optimally responsive” team for a pre-product/market fit startup seems to be 2-10 people who are all either building the product or talking to users. For tech founders, there are four types of knowledge that are critical: 💻 Technical Knowledge: how to build the thing 💵 Go to Market Knowledge: how to sell the thing 🎓 Subject Matter Expertise: all the things that impact the thing 🗺️ Product Knowledge: how to vision the thing as a finished product, but also in all the iterations of its intermediate stages When I see an early-stage startup with all of these bases covered, I know it’s going to execute well. On the other hand, when I see startups struggling, it’s because the team lacks one of these types of knowledge. They’re waiting until after the MVP to bring in the product person, or they’re trying to capitalize on their traction with vibecoding, or they believe good products sell themselves (they don’t). Or they’re trying to backfill knowledge gaps with constant LLM queries that lack the context, experience, and moral compass for reliable decision-making. You can get by for a little while without one of these, but not for long. Any serious early-stage founder should be looking to line up a knowledge source for each of these areas as part of their growth strategy. All four types of knowledge don’t have to live in one person, or even on payroll. 86 percent of authors don't know this basic truth… Books need hooks. Even if they're made-up statistics. A hook does exactly what it says. It catches the reader's attention at the start of the book and again the start of each chapter. It tells them what they're going to read about and why they should be interested. Hooks come in many forms. 🎣 An anecdote that makes the reader want to know what happens next: "When my team leader called me into her office, I had no idea that the meeting would change my life." 🎣 A striking quotation that makes the reader's curious about how it fits your argument: "I find that the harder I work, the more luck I seem to have." Thomas Jefferson's words should be written on every founder's wall… 🎣 A remarkable statistic that takes the reader by surprise: "86 percent of authors don't know this basic trick." (Tip: This works better if the statistic is real.) 🎣 A controversial statement that confirms or challenges the reader's ideas: "Being miserable is part of being an entrepreneur. Get used to it." 🎣 A random fact that stops the reader in their tracks: "In 2010 a British goldsmith engraved the Lord's Prayer on the head of a pin." The hook needs to be long enough to do three things: • Get the reader's attention • Introduce the rest of the chapter • Get out of the way and let the chapter start Finding a hook isn't always easy … but once you find it, you're halfway to planning a great chapter. Scaling Stripe with Patrick Collison — Class 11 Notes of Stanford University’s CS183C

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `
Here is an essay version of my class notes from Class 11 of Stanford University’s CS183C — Technology-enabled Blitzscaling— taught by Reid Hoffman, John Lilly, Chris Yeh, and Allen Blue. Errors and omissions are my own. Credit for good stuff is Reid, John, and Allen’s entirely.
This class was an interview by John Lilly, of Patrick Collison— the founder and CEO of Stripe — on lessons learned from scaling Stripe.
John Lilly: What is the founding story of Stripe?
Patrick Collison: I am skeptical about the “founding mythology” but I can share the story of how we started Stripe.
I started Stripe with my brother John Collison while we were in school together. We first started off building iPhone apps together and using the money we made from them to pay our tuition.
We got into the habit of working on side projects together early and realized it was easy to make money on iPhone apps (this was in the early days of the app store) and one of the things which made it so easy was the ability to charge and pay for things easily.
We were talking about why it was so much more difficult to charge for things online vs. on the app store — most websites made it a huge pain to pay for things. At the time there was this company called Slicehost which was the first really good virtual hosting provider — it made getting a server very straightforward. Our original idea was why don’t we build Slicehost for payments.
Originally we were just going to build a prototype and assumed it wouldn't be too hard. 5+ years later we are still at it — you can call it the world’s longest “ yak shave.”
In 2010, we built and released the first version of Stripe — actually, back then it was named /dev/payments (we weren’t great branding experts). We launched it to a few friends to get enough of a sense for the basic experience of charging for credit cards. We just put up a wait list on our site and demand took off, over a couple months we had a huge wait list. We weren’t expecting it.
John Lilly: You created the first version and then how did people find out about it? What kinds of people were signing up?
Patrick Collison: I had been involved in the LISP programming community for a very long time. It’s a very small community and this is when I got to know Paul Graham. We got into YCombinator and built this payment API for developers — many of our friends were doing startups so we had a strong sense on what the community wanted.
We just started showing it to people and getting feedback. All of our first 20–30 users came from YC companies or friends we knew. From there people talked to each other about it — payments were a big pain point.
John Lilly: One of my rules of thumb is put yourself around people who are doing interesting things — then when you do interesting things, other people can see and use it.
What is it like to start a company with your brother?
Patrick Collison: It was good because we were really good at resolving our differences, we had 20 years of fighting between us as experience.
It’s a common case with high growth startups where the co-founding team breaks up — generally it's hard to get the team to persist. It’s easy to stick with it when you have known the person for decades either as a friend or family.
It’s inevitable that tough situations will come up, but it’s how you react that is the challenge.
John Lilly: You also are known for reading a lot of history (including physical books), why do you think it's important to study history?
Patrick Collison: It’s a way to cheat. You could try to pound your head against the wall and think of original ideas — or you can cheat by reading them in books.
If you look at people like Douglas Engelbart— he didn’t just invent the mouse, it was so much more than that.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yJDv-zdhzMY
In his famous video “the mother of all demos” — he invented real time collaboration (Etherpad), video conferencing, live syncing of documents with video conferencing. The way I see it, he invented a better “Google Hangouts” system — and this was in 1968.
What’s surprising is so much of this early work is better than what we have today. Sure we have solved all kinds of scaling issues, deployments, technology — but the core problems of helping people work together were thought about more back then. Their concept of technology was the empowering of humans through technology.
So much of software today is either about 1) basic utility or 2) entertainment vs. the enabling of people. It’s much harder to see the latter. One phrase we use at Stripe is “most tech companies are building cars, Stripe is building roads.”
John Lilly: How big is Stripe now?
Patrick Collison: We are at 330 people and we are around 5 years old now depending on how you count when Stripe started — we launched on September 30, 2011 but had the idea in 2010.
John Lilly: And a year ago Stripe was half this size?
Patrick Collison: Yes a year ago we were 160 people, we're doubling every year.
John Lilly: Can you talk about how Stripe is organized? Do you have VP’s, managers, etc?
Patrick Collison: We are conventionally organized. As founders we have a tendency to do things differently. It comes from a good place but there are two problems with innovating on the organizational model:
1. The standard means of organization helped create Google/Facebook/Amazon/etc. Even if you could organize a company in a better way, do you need to do that?
2. Any alternative you do create, it's hard to get exposure and experience in that model.
Technology tends to change a lot but people and the organization of people are the same systems today.
However, there are areas for improvement. For example with interviewing, everyone copies how other companies interview but even Google has learned that there is no correlation between GPA and performance at the company. Algorithmic style coding interviews using whiteboards don’t work — and even then no one does anything different — this is crazy.
We don’t do this. When people interview at Stripe they write code using their own laptop in any language they like.
John Lilly: Stripe has been known for being excellent at hiring, how long did it take for you guys to move towards interviewing with personal laptops?
Patrick Collison: It’s always hard to know if things are good or bad, or what to attribute success to.
One of our biggest benefits is we were building software for engineers therefore we had a higher affinity towards engineers — how much did this matter? It’s hard to know.
The biggest thing for us was being ok with taking a really long time to hire great people. It took us 6 months to hire the first 2 people at Stripe. The next 6 months after that we hired 3–4 people. We did week long trials with many people and many people didn’t want to join after that.
In some cases we were having 3 month long conversations with people just to see if they would be interested in joining. When you are trying to hire great people there are two options:
1. Filter by expressed interest — secondary filter for who is good or not.
2. Find great people and then convert them to expressed interest.
We took the second approach but it was difficult. Multiple people at Stripe took several years to hire, 5 people took 3+ years to hire. The idea of taking 3+ years to hire someone is crazy, our biggest thing was being way more persistent than others and being ok with taking longer than any reasonable person would.
John Lilly: It sounds like you had product market fit right at the beginning? Was that true?
Patrick Collison: Yes and no.
Yes, in the very beginning — the idea of a credit card API and being easy to implement it in your own product — turned out to be a good idea.
No, from the fact that all of the additional products where we saw real product market fit — happened on top of the first layer we built. For example — Stripe connect — was a product we build for marketplaces (Instacart, Shyp, Postmates, Lyft, Uber) which helps them facilitate transactions between the people on their platforms. The vast majority of these marketplaces are not built on stripe — we had to produce all of these additional API’s on top of our original transaction layer we built.
John Lilly: It sounds like there were successive product market fits spread out over time — fast forward to 2015 — what scale are you at today? How do you talk about scale?
Patrick Collison: We don’t really talk about numbers but we help transact billions of dollars a year and have 100,000’s of businesses on the platform.
John Lilly: When you talk about Stripe Connect, isn’t this the same as PayPal? A peer to peer (P2P) payment network?
Patrick Collison: On the surface level it looks the same.
When you look one level deeper however the critical difference is what Lyft needs to facilitate transactions is very different than what you need to pay your friend.
Lyft is a business mediating the payment — what happens when a ride gets refunded, dealing with tips, chargebacks, different currencies, etc. It’s a very different problem set.
Question from the audience: How did you set up the relationships early with banks?
In the beginning we worked with a friend who ran a payments company in the midwest, we basically built our prototype on top of his platform. In fact in the early days we were processing many of the payments by hand manually.
This didn’t scale however so we asked one of our investors Geoff Ralston to help us with this problem. Jeff had previously started LaLa one of the first legitimate music services which set up relationships with all of the music labels.
He introduced us to Billy Alvarado who actually joined Stripe when we were a 5 person company. He was a business development person — not an engineer — and we were very torn over this decision. The team thought “he doesn’t write code, what is he going to do?”
After much anguish we hired him and he has become one of the most important people in the company. He solved all of our early banking relationship problems and helped teach us how startups could partner with traditional companies.John Lilly: Given Stripe had a clear value proposition from the early days — how did you decide what new things to do?
Patrick Collison: This is a tough question. Customers typically don’t know what they want and it’s difficult the find the answers through analysis.
Luckily when you are developing product developers, you can cheat and just ask them what you want — developers are outspoken and will also ask you directly. Next the challenge is how to prioritize various features. We spend a lot of time getting to know which customers have good judgements about our product.
There is a quote from Mark Zuckerberg which I like on this subject which is “What’s optional for a 1 year time horizon, might not be good for a 5 year time horizon.” It’s our job to have good judgement on how the pieces fit together.
I’d say about 70% of our new product ideas comes from listening to customers with good judgement and 30% are doing things which ought to be big but aren’t being asked for.
John Lilly: How’s Bitcoin related to all of this? Developers get excited about Bitcoin but it doesn’t feel like it’s a thing yet? Huge yet?
Patrick Collison: Bitcoin is definitely not huge yet.
However you can’t judge decisions by their direct outcomes — instead you should judge decisions by their expected value.
For Bitcoin it’s still small but the potential outcome could be significant. In addition we felt some general affinity towards the Bitcoin community. The distributed systems, decentralized infrastructure, and system of ledger are all cool concepts. Also the original Satoshi paper is one of the first papers which is both a CS breakthrough and a political philosophy breakthrough. So we decided to help out a bit.
John Lilly: Who runs product at Stripe?
Patrick Collison: We don’t have separate engineering and product teams within Stripe, everything is broken up by product engineering teams with one manager for each team. Then I manage all of the managers.
John Lilly: I’ve noticed when the CEO and founders are in the details of the product — this creates instability for the VP of product. Either the CEO is the micromanager of product, the CEO lets the VP of Product run with it however they want, or the CEO just doesn’t care. It’s a tough role to be in.
Patrick Collison: I agree it’s a tough role. Given we don’t have a separate product role, I am fairly involved in our product. The head of product helps staff, build, operate, and works with me.
John Lilly: Has there been any products Stripe has launched and killed?
Patrick Collison: Part of the thing that makes this difficult to answer is — product innovation tends to slow down as you scale.
We built the first product with 3 people, now we are at 300, why are we slowing down? Even as a user of Airbnb/Dropbox/etc., as a user I feel like the innovation has stopped.
What happens is so much additional investment is required to keep up with growth — the original 3 people can’t operate the full system anymore, it’s just so much bigger than when it started.
When you are looking to scale and innovate — this requires hiring many people, training lots of people, having them learn in this emergent process, figuring out how to coordinate together, etc. The net effect is product advances slower.
However, if you do a good job at this, you can look back and scale much faster once your systems are in place. Facebook did a good job at this: moved fast, went into a slow period, then moved quickly again.
At Stripe we haven’t launched many discrete new products — we have shipped many features and experiments but we haven’t killed any full new products. Because we have developers as customers — we run many ideas by them in idea/beta phases and if they don't like it, we stop. Before anything gets to launch we have had pretty exhaustive validation.
John Lilly: Moving to a management question, what do you do differently now that you are past 150 people (Dunbar’s number)?
Patrick Collison: Past 150 has been a big change for us. The biggest is a need for formal explicit communication — specifically broadcast community.
This kind of communication feels very unnatural — no one wakes up in the morning and sets out to talk in bullet points — Here is our 3 bullet points for our Q4 strategy. When you think about it, a startup itself is not a natural environment, there are different things you do that you would not do naturally. You don’t typically grow your social circle 50–100% year over year.
All of the discussions and debates we have — many of our current team weren’t there when we had those. 50% of our entire team wasn’t there a year ago. Each new person that comes in wants to do things different. Part of it is good in the fact that it re-opens issues and part of it is bad, given new people don’t have the full context.
John Lilly: One of the profound realizations I had is the CEO learns really fast but the various layers of the company can't possibly absorb all of this change — so I moved towards speaking consistently and getting alignment the bigger we got.
Patrick Collison: It’s delicate because we want alignment but at the same time if it becomes too rigid it will be problematic because people will want to quit. We need the right amount of structure.
One caveat to all of this is I don’t want to be the person to make all of the decisions — I don't want the organization to do everything I want to do — I want them to do the right things.
When I look at “The Stripe way” it’s a distinct thing and a distinct way of doing things. Sure I am close to it but it’s changing and evolving with all of the people we bring on.
One thing I have noticed for myself is a general shift from speaking to writing. It’s not that I need to speak less but I need to add more writing to my communication. Speaking can only happen once but writing can persist through time, be revised, be updated, and help in clarity.
One of my favorite insights which has had a big impact is a paper from Bruno Latour about the scientific revolution. His argument was the printing press was critical but it’s not because the printing press led to more distribution.
It was because written word was more concrete and rigid. Previously most scientific information traveled through spoken word — but when you are hearing about something from a friend, of a friend, of a friend — who knows how much the facts have shifted. It’s tough to know what is true vs. not.
In writing you can point to paragraph 3, line 2 and can disagree with that point specifically.
Question from the audience: How has the transition from developer to manager been for you?
Patrick Collison: It still pains me, I miss coding. I get this question a lot and yes I miss it a lot.
I think our industry over biases towards founders and the CEO — the media wants to associate a company with the person. The degree in which I am representing of Stripe is overrepresented compared to everyone one. It wasn’t just my transition to management — it was the whole team's transition to a large company.
(I didn’t write down who Patrick was quoting but) Basically the CEO’s job can be reduced to three things:
- Strategy
- Culture (No other person besides the founder/CEO can affect culture to the same degree)
- Selecting senior management of the company (hard for anyone else to do this job) — these people will be the domain experts of their function who are better than you.
- Optional is the product — the CEO can be the head of product or lead some specific function of the product.
That’s it. It’s easy if you can get these pieces right, it’s very difficult to fix things when they aren’t working. It’s a bit simplistic but it’s been helpful for me.

NT: Stripe is a an extraordinary company. You have two products that most many people know. One is payments processing. In fact, 20 percent of all new technology companies incorporated in Delaware are through Stripe Atlas.

NT: It’s the first question of the weekend!

In her terms, capitalism represents a kind of mistaken nomenclature, and that actually the core of capitalism is opportunity. And so I think Stripe is a kind of digital, online version of that. What we're trying to do is to sort of build economic infrastructure for the Internet and to sort of try to create opportunities for people to build products, implant business models, expand globally to take something that they can build in their bedroom and then deploy it at full planetary scale. And it’s important to do so in such a way that the the most important tools and the most sophisticated advanced functionalities are available, not only to the biggest companies in the world, but also, I mean, to the two people starting out in their bedroom.
You mentioned some of the really large companies that use Stripe. But what I love about their use of Stripe is that in almost every case, they're using the same version of Stripe that, you know, an Atlas founder in Nigeria just starting out can take advantage of. And so, internally as we articulate Stripe’s long-term goals, one of—the third one in fact—is that we try to be an anti-incumbency force. We want to kind of reduce activation energy barriers. We want to make incumbency less of a competitive advantage. I mean we're still a small company in the scheme of things. But on net, we're arming the upstarts.
NT: The anti-incumbency bias is a super interesting point. And I understand that you do help the incumbents, but you help them with the same code that you help the Egyptian startups. But how far would you take this though? Stripe’s getting pretty big. You're taking an ever-larger portion of the payment space. When do you become a dominant incumbent? And when you reach that position, do you worry about the effect you'll have on the economy? We have a monopolist-centered tech economy. You're going to become one of those at your current trajectory. PC: OK, so three points. First, part of the reason we have kind of Stripe being an anti-incumbency force as being one of our explicit, written-down, long-term goals is so that we're kind of bound to it. And so we can't decide if and when we become sort of some much larger pillar of the economy, that actually, hey, you know, those upstarts aren't so great after all. Let's just sort of hope the cozy club of the entities which prevail around us in that moment.
Second is I think that, I'm not sure this narrative of tech monopoly is true. I don't want to say that it's completely untrue, but if you look at it just kind of a tech sort of market cap concentration, the tech industry does not appear to be more concentrated today than it was 10 years ago, 20 years ago, 30 years ago. NT: Certain verticals get more concentrated, but there's so many more verticals, the total amount of concentration stays the same?
PC: Not even that. If you just look S&P 500 a percentage of the technology sector occupied by the top four technology companies, that does not seem to be more concentrated than it was in the past. However there is something, I think, important going on here, which is economy wide, we are definitely seeing much more concentration. And so, you know, there are kind of various studies around this, but you just take the median industry by either market cap or by revenue is getting more concentrated. And anecdotally we see this. I mean, you look at it globally—semiconductors, right. Thirty years ago, there were many different kinds of semiconductor companies. And now you know that there's really only three meaningful ones, not just in the U.S. but sort of on a global basis.

And then you have those nuanced questions around, well should we be looking at revenue concentration, or should we be looking at monopoly pricing? And to your point they were so carefully thought through and that the scale and variance and all the rest is really so amazing. And what’s so amazing about the Internet is, like, these guys invented in the late 60s 70s 80s and so on. But you know it is very funny to go back and look at the early error codes in the HTTP specification. There is this 404 we're all familiar with. There's lots of other aspects as well. Alan Kay talks about how remarkable a culture and singular the group was that invented the core protocols of the Internet. You know the folks at Park and MIT and this Cambrian explosion of innovation. And so I guess the question I find myself thinking about and wondering about is, well was there a particular ethos and culture and sort of set of connections and individuals and kind of sets of you know mentors and inspiration they had that really served us well in, say, the 25 years before 1993. And so the fact that I was making this other argument was kind of a reason he should invest in Stripe. And incidentally the Chinese podcast economy appears to be sort of quite a bit more lucrative—and there’s all this other stuff you have to normalize for, now what are they competing against and so on. NT: Right.
PC: But I'm biased.
NT: Alright. Last question since we're almost out of time. WIRED started in these offices because Silicon Valley was blowing up around these offices. We moved here in 1994. PC: I think that the returns agglomeration are very high right. And you know Paul Romer just won the Nobel Prize and he's obviously one of the foremost scholars of this. And so I think, absent self sabotage, Silicon Valley should—I don’t mean normatively should—remain substantially preeminent. It obviously has had kind of really remarkable durability since the 30s, or since Fairchild, or whatever.

It's becoming ever harder for people who aren't benefiting from the opportunities in Silicon Valley to come here and to do so. And then there is an international mobility barrier. Immigration laws and rules have always been bad—or at least, they’ve never been great on the margins and maybe getting slightly worse. I also think the comparative advantage of the U.S. is diminishing.
And then thirdly, a kind of cultural change. Others can decide whether it's for good or ill. But you know I was just talking to Louis and he was just saying how today in Silicon Valley those who are positive, those who are optimistic, they are now the revolutionaries and the counterculture. And that's a remarkable statement. We're supposed to be the epicenter of innovation in the world. And I think it's so easy sitting here—San Francisco is rarely accused of being kind of a representative example of the sort of what it's like to exist you know in just some random place in the world. It is, you know, San Francisco is not normal. And I think in many ways that's good. But the downside is that, you know, since WIRED was founded, the size of the global middle class has tripled. And now more than half the world's population is in the middle class. Everyone here is familiar I'm sure with the sort of the endless statistics about how kind of things are getting better, and yet somehow kind that is not internalized in our mood. The mood is somehow that things are getting worse. And I think sort of a central question for the next 25 years is where does that mood go? Because you know ideology and symbolism and aspirations really matter.
NT: Alright. Well, my mood is better having listened to this man for 20 minutes. So thank you very much. We will see you at Wired 2043.

Stripe CEO Patrick Collison shares the tactics he used for finding product/market fit “We tried very hard to understand in granular detail what exactly it was that people were doing, where they were tripping up and so on.” Patrick gives some examples of specific tactics: • A public chat room to provide support to people integrating Stripe • For the first 10 users of Stripe, every API request sent an email to the founders so they could better understand how users were using their product and see if users were doing anything weird • All errors generated a high-priority email to the founders. This created a pleasant user experience where 15 minutes after hitting an error, Patrick could reach out to them and let them know the issue was fixed “These are all kind of examples of a general pattern of trying to be hyper-attentive to all the micro details of what people were doing in the product and iterating rapidly in response to it. Generally speaking, I think pre-product/market fit metrics are actually relatively unhelpful because probably not that many people are using your product. If it’s 20 users, you can in some sense afford to just look at everything they’re doing to understand what’s working and what isn’t.” Another example of this Patrick gives is embedding a text input on each of their web pages with placeholder text prompting users to give them useful feedback(e.g. “The worst thing about Stripe is…”, “The worst thing about this page is…”, or “I really hate the way Stripe does…”). As Patrick explains: “At that stage, you have to be kind of masochistic. We’d always be waking up to all these emails telling us all the terrible things about Stripe. But that was a helpful to-do list for the day ahead. Video source: Y Combinator(2018)

Patrick Collison on what he wishes he did differently when scaling Stripe “I think one of the most pernicious mental models you can have is that you are on some growth curve… I think a much better mental model to have is that you’re serving some market, and then there’s the percentage of the market that you’re serving. And whatever percentage you are not serving, you just haven’t built the go-to-market functions and organization that’s brought the product to those market segments.” In the early days of Stripe, they assumed they were on some growth curve, but Patrick believes they could’ve accelerated their growth by viewing growth as a function of their go-to-market apparatus: “What we did not do, but what I wish we did, is six months after launch, we should’ve mapped out the concentric circles of our market.” As he suggests, Stripe should’ve started with very early stage startups then mapped out the larger set of all technical startups - not necessarily very early stage. Then continue this process in successive increments until they got to all companies handling online payments. Each step along the way, figuring out the size of each market, the fraction Stripe was currently serving, what it would take to serve more, and so on. And then work backwards from there: “What would the organization look like that was serving the entire market? Let’s just start building that organization, because the growth curve is under my control. Of course, it’s not 100% under your control, but I think it’s much more under your control than people tend to think.” Video source: Y Combinator(2018)

Two Years. 50 Users. The Painful Secret Behind Stripe’s Explosive Growth. John Collison remembers it vividly. “We started Stripe in 2009 and launched in 2011. I told Patrick, ‘How hard can it be?’ The answer: two years of difficulty.” Two years later, Stripe had just 50 users. Not 50,000. Not 5,000. Fifty. For most founders, that would feel like failure. For the Collison brothers, it became their greatest competitive advantage. “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like progress,” John admits. “But even a good idea can feel slow-going.” Here’s the paradox of progress: slowness can be strategic. Because Stripe wasn’t scaling fast, they could obsess over every user: • Every bug report triggered an immediate personal response. • Customers could message the founders directly, 24/7. • Bay Area users were invited into the office to integrate Stripe side-by-side with the team. • Feedback on the dashboard got a reply in under 10 minutes. That obsessive care created what John calls a “surprising viral effect.” Each delighted user told another. Word of mouth became Stripe’s growth engine — one that still powers it today. “The thing that allowed us to take off later,” John reflects, “was the fact that we spent so much time on those first users. We built the product we truly wanted — and a culture of taking abnormally good care of our customers.” What looks like slow growth from the outside often hides the foundation of long-term scale. The lesson: Don’t confuse early stillness for stagnation. Sometimes, the quiet years are the ones that build enduring companies. Oluwasoye P. Most founders think “grow faster” means “do more.” It doesn’t. It means pause, think, and then do better. Stripe didn’t scale because they hired faster. They scaled because they got smarter about how they operated. Here’s what their COO, Claire Hughes Johnson, figured out (and what most founders miss): When you grow fast chaos sneaks in disguised as “momentum.” You hire. You ship. You sprint. Then one day, you wake up and realise: Your company’s making decisions you wouldn’t have made yourself. That’s when the real work starts. To grow intentionally, not accidentally, Stripe asked 6 hard questions every founder should steal: 1️⃣ Have we documented how we make decisions? ⇢ If it’s not written down, it doesn’t scale. Period. 2️⃣ Do we have a structure that actually supports our goals? ⇢ Flat orgs sound cool until no one knows who’s accountable. 3️⃣ Who’s been successful here and why? ⇢ Pattern match what works. Multiply it. 4️⃣ Do we have a 5-year plan that’s simple and alive? ⇢ If your people can’t recite it, it’s useless. 5️⃣ Do we know how our people feel? ⇢ Engagement drives performance. Disconnection kills it. 6️⃣ Are we decentralising decisions? ⇢ Because the companies that win are the ones that teach everyone how to think like founders. Growth isn’t about adding more people, products, or features. It’s about building systems of trust and clarity that let everyone move faster without you. You don’t scale by pushing harder. You scale by creating an organisation that doesn’t need you to push.

Most candidates think Big Tech offers all look the same. This just isn't true at all. Google vs. startups is a completely different game, and your strategy has to change. Big Tech Negotiation: ↳ Predictable RSU schedules ↳ Fixed salary bands ↳ Limited base flexibility You’re negotiating inside guardrails. Startup Negotiation: ↳ Everything’s on the table ↳ Wide comp ranges ↳ Equity % swings outcomes massively You have more leverage points. Example: • Google L6 = $400K total comp (steady, cash-like RSUs) • Series B startup = $180K + 0.15% equity (uncertain, but with upside) The math is different, the leverage is different, and the rules are different. Founders lose money when they negotiate like it’s Google while talking to a startup. Smart candidates adjust their strategy to the structure of the offer. Big Tech has policies. Startups have flexibility. Know the difference. P.S. ♻️ Repost and save if you want the startup vs. Big Tech negotiation framework.

John Collison: We only had 50 users two years after founding Stripe “We started working on Stripe in the Fall of 2009, and we launched Stripe in September 2011,” John Collison reflects. “I remember right at the beginning when we were starting it I said to Patrick [Collison], ‘Yeah let’s do it. How hard can it be?’ Which gives you a sense of our mindset. And the answer was: two years of difficulty. We had not predicted that.” John remembers feeling dejected when Stripe only had 50 users two years later: “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like a whole lot of progress. It feels like things are going pretty slow.” But this is one of the challenges of startups, he argues: “If you’re working on a startup that’s a bad idea, it’s going to feel like slow-going. But if you’re working on a startup that’s a good idea, it may feel like slow-going too.” Yet slow growth has a silver lining: “I think the thing that allowed us to take off in the subsequent years was the fact that since we were spending so much time on each one of those users; since we were hyper-focused on building a great product; and since we weren’t dealing with problems of scale yet, that allowed us to build the product that we wanted. Part of the culture that set in really early on was taking abnormally good care of those early users.” The Stripe founders would get an email or phone call anytime a user ran into a bug. When they sent the customer an email moments later alerting them that the bug was now fixed, people’s minds were blown. They set up a Campfire room that any customer could join and use to message John and Patrick at any hour of the day or night. And if a user was based in the Bay Area, the founders would invite them to come by the office and help integrate Stripe for them. In the Stripe dashboard they would prompt their customers for feedback and feature requests. Then the Stripe founders would reply to that feedback within 10 minutes. “What this meant was that even though the user growth was happening quite slowly in the early days,” John explains, “it actually had a pretty surprising viral effect where people had a good experience, they told their friends about it, and we were able to spread entirely through word-of-mouth even to this day.” Video source: Stanford Technology Ventures Program (STVP)(2015)

My reaction when acquaintance & strangers tell me (not even ask) - "What you're building at NextDoor.Company, any big tech can easily put a map and do what you do." I just smile and go back to building. Because this is the most common fear led, risk averse statement people make when they see something new that doesn't fit the existing ecosystem. When Airbnb came out, people said "who will book a stranger's home across the country when hotels exist?" When Uber launched, people said "who will get into a random person's car when taxis are regulated and safe?" When Stripe started, people said "PayPal already exists, and banks have been doing payments forever." When Notion began, people said "we already have Google Docs, Evernote, and a thousand note taking apps." Here's what I've learned after 13 years of building products and doing 0 to 1: Big tech sure can copy anything. They have unlimited resources, the best engineers, and massive distribution. But they usually end up loosing a few key things while they were growing to become this behemoth. 1. Unwavering user centricity: They stop obsessing about core user problems and become enterprise serving orgs (exceptions exist and cases differs) 2. Speed: They introduce hierarchy and processes which is anti-speed. A simple feature takes months to deploy. 3. Agility: They're so big that they can't just enter any market. Their lane is defined. They can't look in smaller lanes. A company in Series B is looking to get Series C which means high growth, high ticket sizes etc. Big tech builds for scale and shareholders. Startups builds for people with real problems (at least for the first few months and years. And the lucky ones get to retain this DNA and keep doing it for decades) And here's the thing that people miss: by the time big tech notices your idea is worth copying, you've already built relationships, iterated 50 times based on real user feedback, and created something that actually solves the problem in a way only you could. The "big tech can copy you" argument is just another way of saying "don't try." It's the same energy as "don't start a company because 90% fail" or "don't leave your job because it's risky." Yes, it's risky. Yes, someone with more resources could theoretically build what you're building. But they won't build it with your care, your understanding, or your commitment to the specific people you're serving. So, if your idea is good enough that folks say "big tech could copy it," congratulations. You're onto something real. Now go build it before you talk yourself out of it. 📸: From a recent panel discussion at uxindiaconf (ux-india.org)

Startups love to preach “move fast,” but half the stress in early-stage life comes from living three quarters into the future. You’re pitching a Series A in your head while debugging the MVP… worrying about next year’s hiring plan before you’ve even hired the first engineer… or refreshing your Stripe dashboard like it’s a glucose monitor. Here’s the truth: Momentum is built in the present. The next milestone only happens if you nail today’s tiny, unglamorous tasks. And staying in the moment actually helps your health and your company: You sleep better (less imaginary investor drama). You think clearer (no mental tabs open for “what-ifs”). Your team sees calm, not cortisol. So instead of forecasting Q2 2026, try this: Ship something small today. Call the candidate who ghosted you. Drink water that’s not in a coffee cup. Because the only thing worse than burning out… is burning calories worrying about things that haven’t even happened yet.

"I'm also talking to Google"; the startup recruiter's face falls. You just killed your startup negotiation. Here's why: Negotiation is like dating. Don't bring Henry Cavill to your date with a normal person. When you tell a Series B startup " Google offered me $350k," they think: → "Why would you ever choose us?" → "They'll leave the moment Google calls back" → "We can't compete, so why try?" Game over. Here's the rule that actually works: → Match your leverage to your audience. → Startup? Reference other startups. → FAANG? Reference other FAANGs. → Mid-stage? Reference other mid-stage companies. Real example: Robert was negotiating with a Barcelona startup. Instead of mentioning his Google offer, he said: "I'm excited about this role. I'm also in final rounds with [Similar Series B] and [Another European Startup]. Based on those conversations, I'd need to see a package around $X to make this move." Result? → 15% higher base salary → 0.25% more equity → Better contract protections The startup felt they could win. So they fought for him. The psychology: ✅ Credible - they believe you'd actually join ✅ Competitive - but within their league ✅ Respectful - you're not trying to intimidate Wrong leverage = dead negotiation Right leverage = bidding war Next time you negotiate, ask yourself: "Am I bringing Superman to a normal person date?" Have you ever accidentally used the wrong leverage and watched an offer disappear? P.S. I'm hosting a free live workshop on Wednesday at 3:00 PM PST all about crafting a successful counteroffer. In 2012, Patrick Collison, co-founder of Irish payments technology company Stripe, said it would have been too difficult to set up the company in Ireland, because of the banking and investment systems and the talent pool here. More than a decade on, LinkedIn News Europe asked his brother, fellow Stripe co-founder, John Collison, if this would still be the case. For fintechs in particular, John Collison says, the regulatory environment has become easier, and startups in the space in both Ireland and the UK have been embraced, such as Revolut or Monzo. And while the talent pool is bigger than it used to be, it can be challenging for startups to keep hold of people. Apple, Meta, Google and many other US tech titans have set up their European headquarters in Ireland. Asked if the large number of tech giants help or hinder Ireland's startup scene, John Collison said they have a positive impact overall. "You're seeing a great training-up effect of people where they've experienced working in tech," he says . "There's a larger pool to hire from. We see this at Stripe where we hire people in our Dublin office and then they go off and work at a startup there. We would love it if they stayed working at Stripe but we're also very happy for them that they end up working at a startup and contributing to the Irish internet economy that way." See John Collison's full answer on Ireland's regulatory and funding environment for startups below, and read the rest of the interview here: https://lnkd.in/gSBgCyRQ✍: Polly Dennison and Sam Shead📹: Solange Uwimana Source: https://lnkd.in/gMp44Qi5

Two Years. 50 Users. The Painful Secret Behind Stripe’s Explosive Growth. John Collison remembers it vividly. “We started Stripe in 2009 and launched in 2011. I told Patrick, ‘How hard can it be?’ The answer: two years of difficulty.” Two years later, Stripe had just 50 users. Not 50,000. Not 5,000. Fifty. For most founders, that would feel like failure. For the Collison brothers, it became their greatest competitive advantage. “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like progress,” John admits. “But even a good idea can feel slow-going.” Here’s the paradox of progress: slowness can be strategic. Because Stripe wasn’t scaling fast, they could obsess over every user: • Every bug report triggered an immediate personal response. • Customers could message the founders directly, 24/7. • Bay Area users were invited into the office to integrate Stripe side-by-side with the team. • Feedback on the dashboard got a reply in under 10 minutes. That obsessive care created what John calls a “surprising viral effect.” Each delighted user told another. Word of mouth became Stripe’s growth engine — one that still powers it today. “The thing that allowed us to take off later,” John reflects, “was the fact that we spent so much time on those first users. We built the product we truly wanted — and a culture of taking abnormally good care of our customers.” What looks like slow growth from the outside often hides the foundation of long-term scale. The lesson: Don’t confuse early stillness for stagnation. Sometimes, the quiet years are the ones that build enduring companies. Oluwasoye P. Stripe's most controversial acquisition created a $100M+ growth engine nobody saw coming In 2017, Stripe acquired Indie Hackers for an undisclosed sum, and the internet lost its mind. Why would a VC-backed payment giant buy a platform celebrating bootstrapped independence? It seemed like a contradiction. It wasn't. The backstory: • Indie Hackers was founded in 2016 by Courtland Allen to showcase profitable, self-funded businesses • Just 8 months after launch, it was generating $8K/month • Patrick Collison (Stripe CEO) personally reached out after discovering it on Hacker News The controversy was immediate: "This is peak hypocrisy! A VC-backed company buying the symbol of bootstrapped independence?" But Stripe's strategy was brilliant: 1. Keep Indie Hackers completely independent (no Stripe branding) 2. Provide resources for rapid community growth 3. Play the long game – supporting entrepreneurs creates future Stripe customers The results after 6 years: • 85,000+ newsletter subscribers (10X growth) • Thousands of profitable businesses launched • Most using Stripe as their payment processor • Ecosystem play generated $100M+ in processing volume • In 2023, the founders bought it back (with Stripe remaining an investor) The contrarian lesson: Sometimes the best acquisition strategy isn't about absorbing a competitor or technology – it's about nurturing an ecosystem that naturally feeds your core business. Stripe understood that every successful indie founder would eventually need payment processing. By helping more founders succeed, they created their own customer pipeline. Question: What other companies have made counterintuitive acquisitions that paid off in unexpected ways?

The best founders I know aren't trying to be 10x better. Every founder pitching me obsesses over the same thing: "We're 10x better than the competition." 10x faster. 10x cheaper. 10x more features. Stop optimizing for the wrong game. Start creating your own.

A few years ago, critics mocked Stripe for not going public at $95B. Then the market swung, their paper valuation was marked down to $50B, and the narrative became that they “missed their window.” Today they’re back above $100B, not by ringing a bell on Wall Street, but by running a private process on their own terms. The public markets are no longer the sole scoreboard for company quality. The Collison brothers aren’t playing the old game of “IPO as arrival.” They’re playing a longer game, one where control, optionality, and timing matter more than headlines. Buybacks at this scale signal three things: 1/ Confidence - Stripe is consolidating ownership and strengthening its cap table. 2/ Maturity - A $100B+ private company can manage capital allocation the way a public one would, without the quarterly spotlight. 3/ Shifted norms - The playbook is changing. Great companies no longer prove themselves with a ticker symbol. They prove themselves by executing, compounding, and choosing when (or if) they want public scrutiny. For founders, the lesson is bigger than Stripe. The question is no longer “when do we IPO?” but “how do we keep control while still recycling capital and rewarding all stakeholders?” Stripe has been criticized for waiting. But how many companies could take a $50B markdown and come back stronger?

I've been consistently unimpressed (but feel the opposite about the announcement below for all those who don't make it past the ...), but sadly unsurprised, by how many companies have messaged and dealt with layoffs in the SaaS space over the past few months, especially from companies who shallowly proclaim "employees are family members". Layoffs are hard, and there's probably no perfect way to do it, but there are definitely wrong ways of doing it. One of the most frustrating elements of these layoffs is the lack of transparency, clarity, maturity, and empathy (forgetting and no longer being able to connect to and feel what it is to be an employee not in high-ranking leadership) exhibited in these announcements - usually under the guise of increased costs, the pandemic, or oncoming recession - all while crying on screen or proclaiming that it was unpreventable, inevitable, and unforeseen. The bottomline is that leadership at a company is primarily responsible to shareholders and investors, not employees. And when things get tight, pressure sets in, or mistakes must be corrected and reversed, workforce reductions will almost always happen before financial losses are stomached by investors, shareholders, and leadership. This should not be a surprise to anyone, and is part of the reality on which venture capital, and ultimately capitalism, is built. While that can be frustrating at times, it's the way things are. One of the things I love most about leading product at M1 is how we build ➡️ iterate, learn quickly, and always put clients at the center of everything we do. We really focus on understanding what value the feature brings before we invest at scale. Sometimes, that means releasing something to just one client or to a very limited audience so we can see, in the wild, how it solves real problems. How do we measure success? We love utilizing Sean Ellis score to gauge how much we’ve achieved product-market fit and truly solved customer jobs-to-be-done. If real users tell us they’d be very disappointed to lose the feature, we know we’re onto something. I was inspired to incorporate this approach after listening to several of Lenny Rachitsky podcasts—especially the episode with Jag Duggal from Nubank. Their relentless customer focus and culture of experimentation resonated deeply with me, and helped shape how we operate at M1. Case in point: our Dividend Tracker. We heard from so many clients who wanted a transparent, easy way to visualize their expected dividend income, payout schedules, and portfolio growth. The benefits? It can help long-term investors: • 📈 Track & forecast passive income at a glance • 📊 See detailed dividend histories and upcoming payments • ⏩ Automate dividend reinvestment to maximize compounding • 💡 Make more informed portfolio decisions We shipped the first version, gathered early feedback (and yes, watched those Sean Ellis scores!), iterated, and now—I’m proud to say—the Dividend Tracker is available to all M1 clients on mobile.
